topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into my otherwise quiet afternoon. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake of air that felt like swallowing glass. It was the listing. My old street, the house where I spent every summer, was for sale. A wave of icy resistance washed over me, immediately followed by a hot, defensive flush creeping up my neck. I stared at the grainy photos—the peeling paint, the overgrown garden—and felt a tightening in my chest. *No. Absolutely not.* I clicked the link to see the details, my fingers hovering over the back button, a battle raging silently behind my eyes. I needed to be certain this was a mistake, a glitch in the matrix, but the address was undeniably mine. I closed the laptop with a decisive snap, the sound echoing too loudly in the silence of my room.

[story 2]

I reread the listing three times. Each time, the words felt like sandpaper against my resolve. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles vibrating beneath my skin. It was absurd. After all these years, after the distance, after the life I’d built elsewhere, this place—this ghost of my youth—was suddenly up for auction. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat, half a scoff, half a choked denial. I stood up, pacing the length of my living room, my boots scuffing the hardwood floor. I ran a hand through my hair, feeling the familiar urge to retreat, to bury myself under familiar routines. But then, a flicker of something else—a stubborn refusal to acknowledge the reality of it—propelled me forward. I walked back to the desk, my movements stiff, and typed a frantic search query, determined to find the source of this impossible intrusion.

[story 3]

The screen glowed with the address, and my stomach dropped. It wasn't a prank. The details were real, the photos unmistakable. A slow, simmering anger began to build, hot and viscous in my veins. I leaned back in my chair, crossing my arms over my chest, adopting a posture of absolute dismissal. *This isn't happening.* The thought repeated in a loop, a stubborn mantra against the encroaching panic. I wanted to smash the device, to erase the evidence of this intrusion. Instead, I focused on the mundane: the pattern of the rug, the way the afternoon light hit the windowpane. I forced myself to breathe slowly, deliberately, trying to smother the rising tide of anxiety with sheer, unyielding mental fortitude. I wouldn't let this change anything.
